description: Elderly polypharmacy with orthostatic hypotension and drug deprescribing
transcript:
Doctor: Good afternoon, Mr. Sharma. How are you feeling?
Patient: Not great, doctor. I feel dizzy when I stand up. And my stomach has been upset.
Doctor: When you say dizzy, is it when you first stand up from sitting or lying down?
Patient: Yes, exactly. When I get up in the morning or from the sofa, everything goes black for a second.
Doctor: That sounds like orthostatic hypotension. It could be from your blood pressure medications. Let me review what you're currently taking.
Patient: I have the list here. Amlodipine 10mg, Metoprolol 50mg, Losartan 50mg, Aspirin 75mg, Atorvastatin 20mg, Metformin 500mg twice, Omeprazole 20mg, and that calcium tablet.
Doctor: That's 8 medications. And your blood pressure at home?
Patient: It's been around 105/60. Sometimes even 95/55.
Doctor: That's too low, especially with all these antihypertensives. We're overtreating your BP. I'm going to simplify your regimen. I'll stop the Losartan and reduce the Amlodipine to 5mg.
Patient: Won't my BP go up?
Doctor: With 105/60, we have room to reduce. And the dizziness is a bigger risk for you — if you fall and fracture a hip at your age, that's very serious.
Patient: That's true. I'm 72 after all.
Doctor: Now about the stomach upset — the Omeprazole you're taking can actually cause long-term issues. How long have you been on it?
Patient: Over a year now. The previous doctor started it for acidity.
Doctor: We should try to wean off it. Long-term PPI use can affect calcium absorption and increase fracture risk. I'll reduce it to 10mg and try to stop it in a month.
Patient: Okay, doctor.
Doctor: Also, check your blood sugar regularly. With the Metformin and the reduced food intake you mentioned, we want to make sure you're not going too low.
Patient: My fasting sugar was 98 last week.
Doctor: That's good. Actually, since your sugar is well controlled, we can even consider reducing Metformin to 500mg once a day.
Patient: Really? That would be nice.
Doctor: Yes. Let me also check — are you taking any herbal supplements or over-the-counter medicines?
Patient: My wife buys some ayurvedic tablets. I take those sometimes for joint pain.
Doctor: What are they? Do you know the name?
Patient: I think it's some Ashwagandha and something else. I don't remember.
Doctor: Please bring the bottle next time. Some herbals can interact with your medications. For example, Ashwagandha can lower blood pressure further, which would make your dizziness worse.
Patient: Oh, I didn't know that. I'll stop taking them until I bring them.
Doctor: Good. So to summarize — stop Losartan, reduce Amlodipine to 5mg, reduce Metformin to once daily, taper Omeprazole. Check BP daily for the next 2 weeks. And bring those ayurvedic tablets.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. You've really simplified things for me.
Doctor: That's the goal. Fewer medicines, better quality of life. Come back in 2 weeks.
Patient: Will do. Thank you.

codes.description: Other long term current drug therapy

soap.subjective: 72-year-old male with 8-drug regimen presents with orthostatic dizziness and stomach upset. Home BP 105/60 to 95/55. Fasting sugar 98 mg/dL. Also taking unverified ayurvedic supplements for joint pain.
soap.objective: BP likely low for current regimen. 8 concurrent medications including 3 antihypertensives. Well-controlled diabetes (FBG 98).
soap.assessment: 1. Orthostatic hypotension secondary to polypharmacy (overtreatment with antihypertensives). 2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled. 3. Potential herb-drug interaction risk (Ashwagandha + antihypertensives). 4. Long-term PPI use requiring deprescribing.
soap.plan: 1. Stop Losartan 50mg. 2. Reduce Amlodipine 10mg to 5mg. 3. Reduce Metformin 500mg BD to 500mg OD. 4. Taper Omeprazole 20mg to 10mg, aim to stop in 1 month. 5. Bring ayurvedic bottles for review. 6. Daily home BP monitoring x 2 weeks. 7. Follow-up in 2 weeks.
